subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa - MARSIPAN-AU medical admission criteria
stem: A 16-year-old girl is brought to a regional NSW emergency department by her parents after six months of progressive food restriction and excessive exercise. She is 162 cm tall and weighs 41.0 kg (BMI 15.6, less than the third percentile for age). Her parents report she now eats only steamed vegetables and skips breakfast and lunch. She insists she is fine and walks 2 hours daily despite cold weather. She has not menstruated for four months. On examination she is wearing layered clothing, looks gaunt, and is cooperative but minimises her food restriction. Heart rate is 38 beats per minute supine, blood pressure 84/52 mmHg supine dropping to 70/40 mmHg on standing with a 28 beat per minute rise in heart rate. Temperature is 35.2 degrees Celsius. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. She is alert and oriented. Bedside investigations show a venous glucose of 3.0 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, phosphate 0.62 mmol/L, magnesium 0.68 mmol/L, and an ECG showing sinus bradycardia with a corrected QT interval of 470 ms.

data_table.Vitals: HR 38 supine, BP 84/52 supine to 70/40 standing (postural drop with HR rise 28), Temp 35.2 C, capillary refill 4 sec
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 3.0 mmol/L, K 3.1, PO4 0.62, Mg 0.68, ECG QTc 470 ms

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home with urgent community eating disorder service follow-up within 48 hours
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU and NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit list HR under 40, postural BP drop over 20 mmHg, T under 35.5 degC, and QTc over 450 ms as red flags mandating inpatient medical admission. Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Admit to a paediatric medical ward for cardiac monitoring, slow refeeding, and electrolyte correction under MARSIPAN-AU pathway
options.rationale: Multiple MARSIPAN-AU red flags are present (HR 38, temperature 35.2 C, postural HR rise >20, QTc 470 ms, hypoglycaemia, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia) mandating paediatric medical ward admission with cardiac monitoring, supervised refeeding starting low (around 5-10 kcal/kg/day or per local pathway), and aggressive electrolyte replacement before psychiatric stabilisation per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit.
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Admit directly to an adolescent inpatient psychiatric unit for behavioural therapy, mealtime supervision, and weight restoration
options.rationale: Acute medical instability (bradycardia, hypothermia, electrolyte derangement, hypoglycaemia) must be corrected on paediatric medical ward first; psychiatric units cannot manage acute instability. Would be correct only after medical stabilisation (Source: ANZAED).

options.text: Observe in the emergency short-stay unit overnight for serial vitals only
options.rationale: Short-stay observation does not provide structured refeeding, electrolyte replacement, or sustained cardiac telemetry; her QTc 470 and multiple electrolyte derangements require inpatient. Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Commence high-calorie oral refeeding at 2500 kcal/day immediately in the emergency department
options.rationale: High-calorie refeeding in severely underweight, hypophosphataemic patient precipitates refeeding syndrome; MARSIPAN-AU recommends starting low (5-20 kcal/kg/day). Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: Acute severe anorexia nervosa with MARSIPAN-AU red flags is a medical emergency requiring paediatric ward admission for cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding, and electrolyte replacement, not primary psychiatric admission (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).
key_points: MARSIPAN-AU red flags: HR <40, postural HR rise >20, BP postural drop >20 mmHg, temp <35.5 C, QTc >450 ms, K <3.0, PO4 <0.5
Medical stabilisation precedes psychiatric admission
Refeeding starts low (5-20 kcal/kg/day) with electrolyte and thiamine replacement
Refeeding syndrome triad: hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia
explanation.pearls: QTc prolongation in anorexia is driven by malnutrition and electrolyte loss; correcting K, Mg, and PO4 usually resolves it without antiarrhythmics (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

stem: A 19-year-old woman with a 3-year history of severe restrictive anorexia nervosa is admitted to a metropolitan tertiary hospital with BMI 13.4 after several weeks of minimal oral intake (estimated <500 kcal per day for at least 14 days). Admission electrolytes showed potassium 3.4 mmol/L, phosphate 0.81 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, and corrected calcium 2.32 mmol/L. The team commenced supervised oral refeeding at 1200 kcal/day with prophylactic oral thiamine 300 mg daily, oral phosphate supplements, and a multivitamin per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit refeeding pathway. After 72 hours of advancing intake to 1800 kcal/day, she develops new bilateral lower limb pitting oedema extending to the mid-calf, generalised fatigue, and mild dyspnoea on exertion. She has no chest pain and no orthopnoea. Repeat bloods show potassium 2.8 mmol/L, phosphate 0.38 mmol/L, magnesium 0.58 mmol/L, sodium 136 mmol/L, urea 2.1 mmol/L, creatinine 48 micromol/L, glucose 5.9 mmol/L, and corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L.

data_table.Bloods (day 3): K 2.8 mmol/L, PO4 0.38 mmol/L, Mg 0.58 mmol/L, Na 136, urea 2.1, creatinine 48, corrected Ca 2.18, glucose 5.9
data_table.Examination: Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-calf, mild exertional dyspnoea, clear chest

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Increase caloric intake to 2200 kcal/day to keep up with rising energy demands and continue oral phosphate supplementation alone without intravenous electrolyte replacement
options.rationale: Advancing calories during refeeding syndrome worsens electrolyte shifts; MARSIPAN-AU recommends halting advancement and replacing electrolytes. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit; MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg orally daily for the lower limb oedema and continue caloric advancement as planned
options.rationale: Refeeding oedema is driven by insulin-mediated Na/water retention and capillary leak; loop diuretics worsen hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia and are contraindicated. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Hold caloric advancement, replace intravenous phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, and continue thiamine; treat refeeding oedema conservatively
options.rationale: This is refeeding syndrome (the classic triad of hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia after starting nutrition in a severely malnourished patient). The NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit and MARSIPAN-AU recommend holding caloric advancement, intravenous replacement of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, continuing thiamine 300 mg daily to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and managing refeeding oedema conservatively with leg elevation since diuretics worsen electrolyte loss.

options.text: Cease all oral intake for 48 hours to allow electrolytes to normalise, then restart at 1200 kcal/day
options.rationale: Ceasing nutrition prolongs starvation and worsens prognosis; advice is to continue nutrition (often dropping calories modestly) while aggressively correcting electrolytes. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Transfuse 4 units of 20 percent albumin to address the lower limb oedema
options.rationale: Refeeding oedema is not driven by hypoalbuminaemia and resolves with time and electrolyte correction; albumin transfusion not indicated and carries overload risk. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome features hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, and hypomagnesaemia driven by insulin-mediated intracellular electrolyte shifts when carbohydrate is reintroduced. Management is to hold caloric advancement, replace electrolytes intravenously, and continue thiamine (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).
key_points: Highest risk: BMI <14, >10% weight loss in 3-6 months, minimal intake >5 days
Replace phosphate, potassium, magnesium IV; continue thiamine 300 mg/day
Refeeding oedema is conservative-management (no diuretics)
Wernicke encephalopathy prevention with thiamine is mandatory
explanation.pearls: Phosphate falls because intracellular ATP synthesis surges with carbohydrate refeeding (NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit).

subtopic_detail: Family-Based Treatment (Maudsley) for adolescent anorexia nervosa
stem: A 14-year-old girl is reviewed at the John Hunter Children's Hospital outpatient adolescent eating disorders clinic (Hunter New England LHD). Over the past 8 months she has restricted intake and lost weight from 52 kg to 42 kg (current BMI 16.1, BMI z-score -1.8). She is medically stable with normal electrolytes, HR 58, BP 102/64 mmHg, no postural drop, and no QTc prolongation. She lives at home with both parents and a younger sister, is engaged in Year 9 schoolwork, and has supportive parents who can take leave from work to be involved in treatment. There is no history of self-harm, no suicidality, and no comorbid substance use. Her parents ask what evidence-based outpatient treatment is most likely to lead to weight restoration and recovery. She is reluctant but agrees to attend treatment if her parents are involved. The InsideOut Institute and the RANZCP eating disorders clinical practice guidelines both inform local pathways.

data_table.Anthropometry: BMI 16.1, BMI z-score -1.8, weight loss 10 kg over 8 months
data_table.Medical status: HR 58, BP 102/64 mmHg, no postural drop, QTc 410 ms, normal electrolytes
data_table.Psychosocial: Lives with both parents and sister, parents able to be involved, no self-harm or suicidality

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line outpatient treatment?

options.text: Family-Based Treatment (Maudsley FBT) with parents taking initial control of refeeding
options.rationale: FBT (Maudsley) is the first-line evidence-based outpatient treatment for adolescent anorexia nervosa per the RANZCP, InsideOut Institute, and NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit guidelines, particularly when the adolescent is medically stable, lives with capable parents, and has illness duration under 3 years. Phase 1 places parents in charge of refeeding; control is gradually returned to the adolescent in phases 2 and 3.
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

options.text: Individual cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) with the adolescent alone
options.rationale: CBT-ED is first-line for adult bulimia, BED, and adult anorexia, but RANZCP ranks FBT first for adolescent anorexia where family involvement possible. Would be correct in adult anorexia (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

options.text: Olanzapine 2.5 mg orally nightly as monotherapy to address eating disorder cognitions
options.rationale: Olanzapine has modest weight-gain adjunctive evidence in adult anorexia, not first-line for adolescents; psychotherapy (FBT) addresses core illness. Would be correct only as adjunct in adults (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Inpatient psychiatric admission for behavioural weight restoration
options.rationale: Medically stable with supportive family; outpatient FBT preferred with admission reserved for medical instability or FBT failure. Would be correct only with medical instability (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Supportive counselling from a school counsellor without family involvement
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling lacks structured refeeding framework FBT provides; classified as inadequate for AN. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: Family-Based Treatment (the Maudsley approach) is the most evidence-based outpatient treatment for medically stable adolescent anorexia nervosa, with about half of adolescents achieving remission and another 25-30% partial response at 12 months. The model has three phases: phase 1 (weight restoration) places parents in complete control of all meals, planning and supervision, externalising the eating disorder as 'an illness' rather than the adolescent's choice; phase 2 (returning control) gradually hands food autonomy back to the adolescent as weight rises and rigidity reduces; phase 3 (adolescent issues) addresses identity, autonomy and relationships once weight and eating are stable. FBT works because it unites parents around a single, time-limited project rather than leaving them paralysed by conflicting advice. Medical stability requires safe vital signs (HR over 50, no orthostatic drop, no electrolyte derangement, BMI percentile permitting outpatient management). Pharmacotherapy has no first-line role in adolescent AN. Olanzapine may have a small role for severe rumination but not for weight gain.
key_points: FBT phase 1: parents lead refeeding
FBT phase 2: control returned gradually to adolescent
FBT phase 3: adolescent development issues addressed
Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for anorexia nervosa
explanation.pearls: Family-Based Treatment (Maudsley FBT) is the first-line outpatient treatment for medically stable adolescent anorexia nervosa: parents take initial control of refeeding (phase 1), gradually return control as weight is restored (phase 2), then address adolescent identity issues (phase 3) (RANZCP; InsideOut Institute).

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

stem: A 22-year-old university student presents to her general practitioner in Newcastle (Hunter New England LHD) with a 2-year history of episodes she describes as eating very large amounts in a discrete period (often a packet of biscuits, a tub of ice cream, and leftover meals) accompanied by a sense of loss of control. Following these episodes she induces vomiting by manual stimulation. The episodes have been occurring on average 4 times per week for the past 6 months. She is preoccupied with body shape and weight, and her self-evaluation is heavily influenced by these. Her BMI is 22.4. She denies food restriction outside binge episodes. She has no suicidal ideation, no significant depressive features, and no substance use. Bloods show potassium 3.2 mmol/L, chloride 92 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, bicarbonate 32 mmol/L, urea 5.0 mmol/L, and creatinine 64 micromol/L. ECG is normal sinus rhythm with QTc 415 ms. She is keen to engage in treatment.

data_table.Binge-purge pattern: Binge then self-induced vomiting, 4 episodes/week for 6 months
data_table.Bloods: K 3.2, Cl 92, Na 138, HCO3 32, urea 5.0, Cr 64

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily as monotherapy without structured psychotherapy
options.rationale: SSRI monotherapy is inferior to CBT-ED; fluoxetine (not sertraline) is only SSRI with bulimia evidence at 60 mg, and adjunctive per RANZCP. Would be correct only as add-on to CBT-ED, with fluoxetine at correct dose (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Olanzapine 5 mg orally nightly to reduce binge urges
options.rationale: Olanzapine is not recommended pharmacotherapy for bulimia; sedation and metabolic effects without efficacy data. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Inpatient psychiatric admission for behavioural disruption of the binge-purge cycle with mealtime supervision and group therapy
options.rationale: Medically stable (mild hypokalaemic alkalosis manageable as outpatient), without suicidality; CBT-ED is delivered in outpatient settings. Would be correct only with medical instability or active suicidality (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) over approximately 20 sessions with oral potassium replacement
options.rationale: CBT-ED (Fairburn enhanced CBT) is first-line for bulimia nervosa per the RANZCP guideline, InsideOut Institute, and Therapeutic Guidelines, typically delivered over 20 sessions across approximately 20 weeks. Her hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting (K 3.2, HCO3 32) warrants oral potassium replacement alongside psychotherapy.
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

options.text: Topiramate 100 mg orally daily as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Topiramate has some bulimia evidence but is not first-line; tolerability and teratogenicity limit use, and CBT-ED outperforms pharmacotherapy. Would be correct only after CBT-ED failure (Source: RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

explanation.summary: CBT-ED is first-line for bulimia nervosa across adolescent and adult presentations per the RANZCP and InsideOut Institute. Fluoxetine 60 mg is the only pharmacological add-on with specific evidence in bulimia and is second-line.
key_points: CBT-ED ~20 sessions over 20 weeks
Fluoxetine 60 mg is the bulimia-licensed SSRI dose (higher than depression dose)
Hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is the classic purging biochemical signature
Sertraline is preferred in pregnancy/lactation but not for bulimia specifically
explanation.pearls: The 60 mg fluoxetine dose for bulimia is double the typical depression starting dose - test favourite (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

stem: A 38-year-old man is referred to a community mental health team at Gosford Hospital (Central Coast LHD) with binge episodes occurring on average 3 days per week for the past 14 months. Episodes involve eating unusually large amounts in under 2 hours, with a sense of loss of control, eating until uncomfortably full, eating alone because of embarrassment, and feeling disgusted afterwards. He does not vomit, use laxatives, fast, or exercise excessively after episodes. His BMI is 34. He has tried two 12-session courses of guided self-help cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) over the past year with only modest reduction in binge frequency, and he has completed a 16-session full CBT-ED course without remission. He has hypertension (treated with perindopril 5 mg daily, BP 128/78 mmHg), no history of cardiovascular disease, no arrhythmia, no glaucoma, no substance use disorder, no past psychosis, and no family history of sudden cardiac death. ECG shows sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms. He asks about pharmacological options to augment further psychotherapy. The Therapeutic Goods Administration has registered one stimulant for adult binge eating disorder in Australia.

data_table.Binge pattern: Binge without compensatory behaviour, 3 days/week for 14 months, DSM-5 BED moderate-severe
data_table.Prior treatment: Guided self-help CBT-ED x2 courses and full CBT-ED 16 sessions, modest response only
data_table.Comorbidities: Hypertension on perindopril, BP 128/78, no CV disease/arrhythmia/glaucoma/SUD/psychosis

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate pharmacological augmentation?

options.text: Olanzapine 5 mg orally nightly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Olanzapine is not registered or recommended for BED and adds metabolic risk in already obese patient. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as binge-suppressing monotherapy without ongoing structured psychotherapy
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is evidence-based at 60 mg for bulimia, not BED; SSRIs have modest effect for BED but lisdexamfetamine has TGA-registered BED indication. Would be correct in bulimia, not BED (Source: AMH).

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally as needed before high-risk binge times
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines not indicated for BED, carry dependence risk, and may disinhibit eating. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Quetiapine 50 mg orally nightly for binge urges and sleep
options.rationale: Quetiapine has no BED indication, causes metabolic weight gain, and worsens obesity comorbidity. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Lisdexamfetamine starting at 30 mg orally daily and titrated, in conjunction with ongoing CBT-ED
options.rationale: Lisdexamfetamine is the only pharmacotherapy with TGA registration for moderate-to-severe adult binge eating disorder in Australia, with robust evidence for reducing binge days per week. Starting dose is 30 mg daily, titrated weekly to 50-70 mg. CBT-ED remains first-line and is continued alongside. His controlled hypertension on perindopril, absence of cardiovascular disease, arrhythmia, glaucoma, substance use disorder, and family history of sudden cardiac death make him a suitable candidate per the Australian Medicines Handbook and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic.
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
TGA Product Information - lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) for BED

explanation.summary: Binge eating disorder is the most prevalent eating disorder in Australia, affecting around 2% of adults across both sexes. DSM-5 diagnosis requires recurrent binge episodes (large amount of food in 2 hours with loss of control) at least weekly for 3 months, marked distress, and three of: rapid eating, eating to fullness, eating without hunger, eating alone from embarrassment, or guilt afterwards. Unlike bulimia, there are no recurrent compensatory behaviours. First-line treatment is enhanced CBT for eating disorders (CBT-ED), with guided self-help an alternative for milder disease. Lisdexamfetamine 30-70 mg daily is the only TGA-registered pharmacotherapy and is used as augmentation to CBT-ED for moderate-severe disease - it reduces binge frequency by about 50% versus placebo. Pre-prescription requirements are baseline ECG, fasting glucose and lipids, BP, HR, weight, and screening for cardiac disease and substance use disorder. Topiramate is an off-label alternative.
key_points: Lisdexamfetamine TGA-registered for adult BED (not for bulimia or anorexia)
Pre-screen: cardiovascular disease, arrhythmia, glaucoma, SUD, family history sudden cardiac death
Starting dose 30 mg/day, titrate to 50-70 mg
CBT-ED remains first-line - pharmacotherapy is augmentation
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine is the only TGA-registered pharmacotherapy for binge eating disorder; titrate from 30 mg to 70 mg daily with BP and HR checks at each visit, baseline ECG, and screening for cardiovascular disease and stimulant misuse risk (AMH; RANZCP BED).

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
TGA Product Information - lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) for BED

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner in Maitland (Hunter New England LHD) with a 4-month history of fatigue, muscle weakness, and occasional palpitations. She is reluctant to discuss eating but eventually discloses, after rapport-building, that she binges 3 to 4 times per week followed by self-induced vomiting. She also occasionally takes stimulant laxatives after large meals. She has not used diuretics. Her BMI is 23.1. Vital signs are HR 92 regular, BP 104/64 mmHg supine with no postural drop, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius. Examination shows parotid swelling bilaterally, scarring on the dorsum of her right hand (Russell sign), and dental enamel erosion on the lingual surface of upper incisors. There is no peripheral oedema. ECG shows sinus rhythm with U waves and a QTc of 472 ms. Bloods show sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, chloride 90 mmol/L, bicarbonate 34 mmol/L, urea 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, magnesium 0.78 mmol/L, and glucose 5.1 mmol/L. Urine chloride is 8 mmol/L.

data_table.Examination signs: Parotid swelling, Russell sign on right hand, lingual dental erosion

lead_in: Which is the single most likely underlying acid-base and electrolyte mechanism?

options.text: Renal tubular acidosis type 1 with hypokalaemic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
options.rationale: RTA-1 produces hypokalaemic ACIDOSIS (low bicarbonate); her bicarbonate is high (34), indicating alkalosis. Would be correct with low bicarbonate (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Self-induced vomiting causing hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic, chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis with secondary hyperaldosteronism
options.rationale: Self-induced vomiting causes loss of gastric HCl, generating metabolic alkalosis (HCO3 34) and hypochloraemia (Cl 90). Volume contraction triggers secondary hyperaldosteronism, producing renal potassium wasting (K 2.9). The classic feature is LOW urine chloride (<20 mmol/L) confirming a chloride-responsive (saline-responsive) alkalosis, distinguishing vomiting from diuretic abuse. Per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology and the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit, this is the signature of purging bulimia.

options.text: Surreptitious thiazide diuretic abuse causing hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with high urine chloride and secondary hyperaldosteronism
options.rationale: Active diuretic use produces HIGH urine chloride (over 20); her urine chloride is 8, consistent with vomiting. Would be correct with urine Cl over 20 (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Primary hyperaldosteronism causing hypokalaemic alkalosis with hypertension
options.rationale: Conn typically has hypertension and suppressed renin; she is normotensive with no primary mineralocorticoid excess features. Would be correct with HTN and suppressed renin (Source: APEG).

options.text: Laxative abuse alone causing hypokalaemic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
options.rationale: Chronic laxative abuse produces ACIDOSIS from colonic bicarbonate loss, not the alkalosis seen here. Would be correct with metabolic acidosis (Source: ANZAED).

explanation.summary: Bulimic and binge-purge subtype eating disorders frequently present with hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis and unexplained palpitations or muscle weakness. The bedside biochemistry distinguishes mechanism: self-induced vomiting loses HCl and water, producing hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with low urine chloride (under 20 mmol/L) and secondary hyperaldosteronism; diuretic abuse produces the same picture but with high urine chloride; laxative abuse produces hypokalaemic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from colonic bicarbonate loss. Beyond electrolytes, look for parotid hypertrophy, dental erosion (lingual surface of upper incisors), Russell's sign (knuckle callus), and oesophageal injury. First-line treatment is enhanced CBT (CBT-ED) plus correction of electrolytes; fluoxetine 60 mg daily is the only TGA-registered pharmacotherapy for bulimia. Lisdexamfetamine is approved for binge eating disorder.
key_points: Vomiting: low Cl, high HCO3, K low, urine Cl <20 mmol/L
Diuretics: similar biochemistry but urine Cl >20 mmol/L
Laxatives: tend toward metabolic acidosis
Russell sign and parotid swelling are physical signs of chronic vomiting
explanation.pearls: In purging behaviours, a urine chloride under 10 mmol/L confirms self-induced vomiting (chloride lost in gastric acid); a urine chloride over 40 mmol/L points to diuretic abuse or laxative use as the driver of the metabolic alkalosis (eTG Endocrinology).

subtopic_detail: ARFID vs picky eating - DSM-5 differentiation
stem: An 11-year-old boy is brought to a paediatric eating disorders clinic at Tamworth Hospital (Hunter New England LHD) by his mother. Since age 4 he has eaten an extremely restricted range of foods, accepting only plain pasta, white bread, banana, and one specific brand of crackers. He refuses foods of certain textures or colours and gags or vomits when pressured to try new foods. He has never expressed concern about body weight, shape, or fear of fatness, and he does not engage in any compensatory behaviours. Over the past 18 months his weight has dropped from the 25th to the 3rd percentile, his linear growth has slowed, and his last iron studies showed ferritin 8 micrograms per litre with microcytic anaemia (Hb 102 g/L, MCV 72 fL). His vitamin D is 28 nmol/L. He has missed multiple school excursions because he refuses to eat unfamiliar food and has lost two friendships over lunchtime exclusion. There is no history of trauma, no obsessive-compulsive disorder, no autism diagnosis (though he is awaiting assessment), and no medical cause for food refusal on prior gastroenterology workup including coeliac serology, endoscopy, and pH probe.

data_table.Eating pattern: Lifelong restriction to 4 foods, refusal based on texture/colour, no weight/shape concern
data_table.Growth: Weight dropped 25th to 3rd percentile, slowed linear growth
data_table.Bloods: Ferritin 8 mcg/L, Hb 102 g/L, MCV 72 fL, Vit D 28 nmol/L
data_table.Psychosocial: Excursion avoidance, social withdrawal at meals

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis?

options.text: Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID) - sensory-based selective intake without weight/shape concern
options.rationale: ARFID per DSM-5 requires persistent failure to meet nutritional or energy needs with one or more of significant weight loss/faltering growth, nutritional deficiency, dependence on enteral feeding or supplements, or marked psychosocial impairment, in the ABSENCE of body image disturbance or fear of fatness. He meets all three sub-criteria (growth failure, iron and vitamin D deficiency, psychosocial impairment) without weight/shape concern, distinguishing ARFID from anorexia nervosa (DSM-5, RANZCP, InsideOut Institute).
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

options.text: Anorexia nervosa, restricting subtype - driven by weight/shape concern and body image disturbance
options.rationale: AN requires fear of weight gain or persistent behaviour preventing weight gain AND body image disturbance; he has neither. Would be correct with weight fear and body image disturbance (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Picky eating within normal developmental range - transient, no nutritional or functional impact
options.rationale: Normal picky eating does not cause faltering growth, nutritional deficiency or psychosocial impairment; his anaemia, growth failure and social withdrawal exceed normal. Would be correct without nutritional impact (Source: DSM-5).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources

options.text: Specific phobia of food (cibophobia) - circumscribed fear of choking or contamination with avoidance
options.rationale: ARFID is the diagnosis when restrictive intake produces nutritional/functional consequences; specific phobia would only apply to circumscribed phobic avoidance. Would be correct in pure circumscribed food phobia without ARFID criteria (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Pica - persistent ingestion of non-nutritive non-food substances inappropriate to developmental level
options.rationale: Pica requires persistent eating of non-nutritive non-food substances; he restricts to narrow food range without non-food consumption. Would be correct with non-food consumption (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: ARFID is the DSM-5 diagnosis for restrictive intake with nutritional or psychosocial impact in the absence of body image disturbance. It commonly presents with sensory sensitivity (texture/colour), low interest in eating, or fear of aversive consequences (choking, vomiting).
key_points: Three ARFID phenotypes: sensory sensitivity, low appetite/interest, fear of aversive consequences
Must cause weight loss/growth failure, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements, or psychosocial impairment
NO body image disturbance (distinguishing from anorexia)
Frequent comorbidity with autism and anxiety
explanation.pearls: Iron and vitamin D deficiency are typical nutritional consequences of ARFID with narrow food range (InsideOut Institute).

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders
InsideOut Institute - Australian National Eating Disorders Strategy and clinical resources
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

stem: A 24-year-old man in Sydney is referred to psychiatry distressed about the appearance of his nose. He spends >=4 hours daily checking his nose in mirrors, takes repeated comparison photos, avoids social outings and dating, and is convinced his nose looks deformed though objectively it is unremarkable. He has consulted two plastic surgeons for rhinoplasty; both declined. Weight, eating and shape concerns are absent. He has no delusions of reference and no auditory hallucinations. He has tried to stop the checking but feels driven to perform it to reduce anxiety. BDD-YBOCS is 28 (severe). He has no current suicidal ideation but past passive ideation when preoccupation peaks. He has no substance use, no psychotic features, K-10 22, no past medication trials and no medical comorbidities. He is keen to start treatment now and has private health cover.

data_table.Preoccupation: Nose appearance, 4+ hours/day, repetitive checking and comparison
data_table.Insight: Recognises behaviours as excessive, feels driven
data_table.Risk: Past passive SI when preoccupation peaks, no current plan

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line treatment plan for severe body dysmorphic disorder?

options.text: Refer for a third rhinoplasty consultation, expecting that successful surgery will resolve the preoccupation
options.rationale: Cosmetic surgery for BDD is a recognised harm anti-pattern; preoccupation typically migrates to a new body part and dissatisfaction recurs. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP BDD).

options.text: Olanzapine 5 mg nocte as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Antipsychotic monotherapy is not first-line; reserved for SSRI-resistant BDD or where delusional intensity is prominent, as augmentation. Would be correct as augmentation after SSRI failure (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: BDD-tailored CBT with exposure and response prevention plus an SSRI titrated to the higher end of the dose range over 12-16 weeks before declaring non-response
options.rationale: Combined ERP-CBT plus an SSRI at the upper licensed dose range (e.g. fluoxetine up to 60-80 mg, escitalopram up to 20 mg respecting the TGA dose cap, sertraline up to 200 mg) is the eTG-endorsed first-line for moderate-to-severe BDD. Adequate trial is at least 12 weeks at maximum tolerated dose before declaring resistance; this avoids premature switching at sub-therapeutic doses, a common pitfall.

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg PRN for distress with no other intervention
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying in BDD and risk dependence in chronic-course illness. Would be correct only as short-term adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Reassurance that the nose looks normal, with no further intervention
options.rationale: Reassurance is ineffective and worsens BDD by reinforcing checking; evidence-based is ERP-CBT plus high-dose SSRI. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: BDD is an obsessive-compulsive spectrum disorder responsive to CBT-ERP plus higher-dose SSRIs. Cosmetic surgery is contraindicated as it does not improve, and often worsens, the underlying preoccupation (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).
key_points: BDD is in DSM-5 Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders chapter
First-line: CBT-ERP + high-dose SSRI
Adequate SSRI trial: 12 weeks at high dose
Avoid cosmetic surgery
explanation.pearls: BDD has high suicide risk; screen actively at every review (RANZCP).

sources.label: RANZCP guidance on body dysmorphic disorder

subtopic_detail: Postpartum psychosis vs PND vs baby blues
stem: A 28-year-old woman, G1P1, delivered her first child by uncomplicated vaginal birth 8 days ago. Her mother brings her to the emergency department at John Hunter Hospital (Hunter New England LHD) because over the past 36 hours she has slept barely 2 hours per night, has been moving rapidly between activities, and tonight told her partner she could hear the baby's deceased grandmother whispering instructions about the baby's safety. She has shifting beliefs that the baby is in danger from neighbours, and she has refused to let her partner near the infant. Examination shows pressured speech, labile affect alternating between elated and tearful, mild thought disorder, and stated auditory hallucinations. She has fleeting thoughts that the baby would be safer elsewhere. She has no prior psychiatric history. Her mother had a hospital admission for a 'breakdown' after her own first child. She is not taking any medications, breastfeeds occasionally, and otherwise the baby is feeding well. Vitals are normal, no infection signs, no fever, basic bloods including TSH, FBC, UEC, LFTs, calcium, and CRP are normal.

data_table.Mental state: Pressured speech, labile affect (elated to tearful), thought disorder, AH commanding (deceased relative), persecutory delusions, fleeting thoughts baby safer elsewhere
data_table.Investigations: TSH, FBC, UEC, LFTs, Ca, CRP all normal
data_table.Family history: Mother psychiatric admission post first child

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Reassurance that this is the postpartum 'baby blues' and discharge home with sleep advice
options.rationale: Baby blues affects up to 80 percent of mothers, peaks day 3-5, resolves by day 10, with lability/tearfulness WITHOUT psychosis or thoughts of infant harm. Would be correct in classic baby blues without psychotic features (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily as outpatient with general practitioner follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis not postnatal depression; SSRI monotherapy is inadequate and outpatient is unsafe given infant safety concerns. Would be correct only in PND without psychotic features (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Reassure that postpartum hormonal fluctuations cause hallucinations and arrange community mental health team review in 72 hours
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is psychiatric emergency with infant safety implications; delaying inpatient risks serious harm. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Urgent psychiatric admission, ideally to a mother-baby unit, for inpatient stabilisation with antipsychotic treatment and infant safety planning
options.rationale: She has classic postpartum psychosis (rapid onset within 2 weeks postpartum, psychotic features, mood lability, fleeting thoughts of infant harm, family history of postpartum psychiatric illness which is a strong risk factor). The COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline and RANZCP perinatal psychiatry guidance treat this as a psychiatric emergency requiring urgent admission, preferably to a mother-baby unit where available, with an antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine or quetiapine), consideration of mood stabiliser, and immediate infant safety planning. Suicide and infanticide risks peak in the first month.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally and overnight ED observation with discharge in the morning if calmer
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine sedation does not address psychosis or infant safety; admission mandatory. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency presenting in the first 2 weeks postpartum with rapid onset, fluctuating psychosis, mood lability, and infant safety risk. Urgent inpatient management (preferably mother-baby unit) is required (COPE Perinatal Guideline).
key_points: Baby blues: days 3-10, mild lability/tearfulness, NO psychosis
Postnatal depression: weeks-months, EPDS >=13, persistent low mood
Postpartum psychosis: <2 weeks postpartum, psychotic features, INFANTICIDE RISK
Past postpartum psychosis or bipolar disorder confers >50% recurrence risk in next pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Family history of postpartum psychosis in first-degree relative is a major risk factor (COPE Guideline).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

subtopic_detail: Lithium and Ebstein anomaly - pregnancy planning in bipolar disorder
stem: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable for 4 years on lithium carbonate 900 mg orally nightly with steady-state 12-hour trough level 0.8 mmol/L, presents to her psychiatrist at Calvary Mater Newcastle (Hunter New England LHD) because she and her male partner are planning a pregnancy within the next 6 months. She has had three previous severe manic episodes (two requiring involuntary inpatient admission with psychotic features) and one severe depressive episode with active suicidal ideation requiring electroconvulsive therapy. Trials of sodium valproate (discontinued due to substantial weight gain of 14 kg over 6 months), olanzapine (discontinued due to metabolic syndrome with HbA1c rising to 50 mmol/mol), and quetiapine (discontinued due to inadequate prophylaxis with breakthrough mania despite a dose of 600 mg daily) have all been documented in her psychiatric file. Lithium has been the only mood stabiliser to achieve sustained euthymia. She has a family history of bipolar disorder (her mother, who also required lithium maintenance) and a maternal aunt with completed suicide. Her renal function is normal (eGFR 95 mL/min/1.73 m squared, urinary albumin-creatinine ratio normal), thyroid function is normal on no replacement (TSH 1.6 mIU/L), and she has no cardiac, renal, or thyroid disease.

data_table.Current regimen: Lithium 900 mg nocte, level 0.8 mmol/L, euthymic 4 years
data_table.Prior trials: Valproate (weight gain), olanzapine (metabolic syndrome), quetiapine (breakthrough mania)
data_table.History: 3 severe manic episodes (2 inpatient), 1 severe depressive with ECT and SI

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discontinue lithium abruptly preconception and continue with no mood stabiliser through pregnancy given TGA category D rating
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium discontinuation precipitates relapse ~50 percent within 6 months, particularly dangerous with severe bipolar and prior ECT. Would never be correct in severe bipolar (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Switch from lithium to sodium valproate immediately as a safer alternative through pregnancy, citing the TGA category D rating of lithium and concerns about Ebstein anomaly
options.rationale: Sodium valproate has HIGHEST teratogenicity of mood stabilisers (~10 percent major malformations including NTDs, IQ reduction up to 9 points); contraindicated in pregnancy. Would never be correct in pregnancy (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine immediately as a safer alternative with a more favourable teratogenicity profile than both lithium and valproate, continue without further foetal surveillance, and avoid foetal echocardiography given the absence of lithium exposure during the planned conception
options.rationale: Carbamazepine carries ~3 percent NTD risk and other malformations; not safer than lithium in severe case with treatment-resistant history. Would be correct only if lithium intolerance and lower-risk profile (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Stop lithium for the first trimester only, then restart in trimester 2
options.rationale: Episodic discontinuation increases relapse without clear advantage; current AU guidance is to continue lowest effective dose with close monitoring. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Continue lithium at the lowest effective dose with twice-monthly level monitoring (more frequent peripartum), arrange a foetal echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks for Ebstein anomaly screening, and plan delivery with peripartum lithium reduction and neonatal monitoring
options.rationale: For severe bipolar disorder with multiple failed alternatives, the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline and RANZCP perinatal psychiatry guidance recommend continuing lithium at the lowest effective dose. Lithium's first-trimester Ebstein anomaly risk is real but lower than historically quoted (around 1 in 1000 to 2 per 1000 absolute risk, relative risk around 10-20 fold above baseline ~1 in 20,000). Management requires twice-monthly lithium levels (more frequent peripartum due to changing volume of distribution), a detailed foetal cardiac echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks, peripartum dose reduction or brief withholding around delivery to prevent neonatal toxicity, and avoidance of breastfeeding on lithium if alternatives exist. Lamotrigine is a consideration for bipolar DEPRESSION prophylaxis but is less effective for mania prevention; in her predominantly manic history, lithium remains optimal.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

explanation.summary: For severe bipolar disorder with prior treatment failures, continuing lithium at the lowest effective dose with intensive monitoring and foetal cardiac surveillance is the safest plan per COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline. The benefit of relapse prevention outweighs the small absolute Ebstein anomaly risk.
key_points: Lithium first-trimester Ebstein risk: ~1-2 per 1000 absolute (relative risk ~10-20)
Valproate is the worst mood stabiliser in pregnancy (10% major malformations, IQ loss)
Lamotrigine is preferred for bipolar DEPRESSION but inferior for mania prophylaxis
Postpartum bipolar relapse rate >50% without prophylaxis
Lithium levels need more frequent monitoring in pregnancy (volume of distribution changes)
explanation.pearls: Lithium dose often falls in pregnancy (rising GFR), then must be reduced rapidly postpartum to avoid toxicity (COPE Guideline).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

stem: A 31-year-old woman attends her 6-week postnatal check at a community general practice in Maitland (Hunter New England LHD). She delivered her second child by lower segment caesarean for breech presentation at 39 weeks. The baby is breastfeeding well and gaining weight along the 50th percentile. The general practitioner administers the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) as part of routine COPE-aligned perinatal mental health screening. The patient scores 14 in total. Her response to question 10 of the EPDS (which asks about self-harm thoughts) is scored 1, corresponding to the response 'Hardly ever'. She reports feeling tearful several days per week, not enjoying activities she previously enjoyed, occasional anxiety about the baby's safety that is manageable, and sleep that is broken but appropriate to a newborn. She denies any plan or intent to harm herself or the baby and describes a supportive partner and engaged extended family. She has no past psychiatric history, no substance use, and no obstetric complications. Her thyroid function tested 2 weeks ago was normal (TSH 1.8 mIU/L). She has no medication allergies. She is not currently taking any medications and is exclusively breastfeeding.

data_table.Mental state: Tearful, anhedonia, manageable anxiety, no active SI/intent
data_table.Obstetric: G2P2, 6 weeks postpartum, LSCS at 39 weeks, breech

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step according to the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline?

options.text: Conduct a structured clinical interview to confirm a depressive episode, offer psychological therapy as first-line, and consider sertraline if symptoms warrant pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: The COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline interprets an EPDS score of 13 or higher (cut-off >=13) as a positive screen suggestive of probable depression requiring further diagnostic assessment. Item 10 must be reviewed at every screen regardless of total; a score of 1 indicates passive ideation requiring direct clinical inquiry but not immediate emergency action. First-line treatment is psychological therapy (CBT or IPT). If pharmacotherapy is needed and she is breastfeeding, sertraline is preferred per the COPE Guideline and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic due to low relative infant dose.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Reassure her that an EPDS score of 14 is within the normal postnatal range for a woman with a newborn, attribute her symptoms to sleep deprivation, and rescreen with the EPDS in 3 months without further action
options.rationale: EPDS 13+ is positive screen requiring further assessment; score 14 with anhedonia and tearfulness should not be dismissed. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Refer urgently to the emergency department because of the item 10 score
options.rationale: Item 10 score 1 (hardly ever) requires direct clinical inquiry to clarify intent/means but does not automatically mandate ED referral. Would be correct only with high item 10 with active suicidality (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg orally daily without further assessment because of EPDS >=13
options.rationale: EPDS is screening tool not diagnostic; diagnosis requires clinical interview, and venlafaxine is not first-line in breastfeeding (sertraline preferred). Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Reassure that the EPDS is unreliable in the first 12 weeks postpartum and defer screening until 3 months
options.rationale: EPDS is validated antenatally and postnatally including early postpartum; COPE recommends screening at 6-12 weeks routinely. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

explanation.summary: EPDS >=13 is the COPE-recommended cut-off for probable depression in Australian perinatal women; item 10 (self-harm) is reviewed independently at every screen. Positive screens require diagnostic interview, with psychological therapy first-line and sertraline preferred if pharmacotherapy is needed during breastfeeding.
key_points: EPDS cut-off >=13: probable depression
EPDS 10-12: possible depression, repeat in 2 weeks
Item 10 always reviewed regardless of total
Sertraline preferred SSRI in breastfeeding (low relative infant dose)
Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate PND
explanation.pearls: EPDS is a screen, not a diagnosis - always confirm with clinical interview (COPE Guideline).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

stem: A 29-year-old woman is 8 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her first child. She has moderate postnatal MDD confirmed after structured interview following EPDS 16; 6 weeks of CBT has produced limited improvement and she wishes to add an SSRI while continuing to breastfeed. She has no past medication trials, no allergies, normal renal and hepatic function, no QTc prolongation (resting ECG QTc 410 ms) and no concerning interactions. The infant was term, is feeding well and gaining weight on the 50th percentile, with no congenital heart disease, no liver dysfunction and no prematurity. The TGA, AMH lactation appendix, and COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline provide concordant guidance. She specifically asks why fluoxetine is not the first choice given a friend was started on it.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line SSRI for this scenario, and why is it preferred over the listed alternatives?

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Fluoxetine and long-half-life norfluoxetine (t1/2 7-15 days) accumulate in breastmilk/infant plasma more than alternatives, producing higher relative infant dose. Would be correct only if started before delivery and well-tolerated (Source: LactMed).

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Sertraline has the lowest relative infant dose (typically <2 percent of weight-adjusted maternal dose), short half-life with no active metabolite of clinical consequence, no QTc dose-cap, and the largest perinatal evidence base. COPE, AMH and RANZCP all list sertraline as preferred first-line in breastfeeding.

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Citalopram has TGA QT-prolongation dose cap and higher relative infant dose than sertraline; not first-line in breastfeeding. Would be correct only if sertraline not tolerated (Source: TGA; LactMed).

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is SNRI not SSRI, and not first-line in breastfeeding; reserved for SSRI failure. Would be correct only after SSRI failure (Source: LactMed).

options.text: Doxepin 25 mg orally nightly
options.rationale: Doxepin is TCA with reported neonatal sedation and respiratory depression in breastfeeding; contraindicated in lactation. Would never be correct (Source: LactMed).

explanation.summary: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in breastfeeding women in Australia, supported by COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic. The relative infant dose (infant intake per kg as percent of maternal weight-adjusted dose) is under 2% for sertraline versus 4-8% for citalopram and escitalopram, and up to 10% for fluoxetine (which has a long active metabolite); paroxetine is also acceptable but is associated with first-trimester teratogenicity if pregnancy recurs. Most infant serum sertraline levels are undetectable at typical maternal doses of 50-150 mg daily. Untreated moderate-severe perinatal depression is associated with significant maternal, infant and family harm - including infanticide and suicide - so the risk-benefit equation strongly favours treatment. Routine infant monitoring is for sedation, feeding difficulties, irritability and growth, with paediatric input if concerns. ECT is safe in breastfeeding.
key_points: Sertraline relative infant dose typically <2%
Avoid fluoxetine (long half-life) and doxepin (infant sedation) in breastfeeding
Citalopram acceptable second-line
Paroxetine has low milk transfer but neonatal adaptation concerns if used in late pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Sertraline has the lowest relative infant dose (under 2%) among SSRIs and the largest perinatal safety database; most infant serum levels are undetectable at typical maternal doses (50-150 mg). Citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine and paroxetine all carry higher RID (COPE; AMH).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

stem: A neonate is born at 39 weeks gestation by spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 30-year-old G2P2 woman with a history of recurrent major depressive disorder, treated throughout pregnancy with paroxetine 20 mg orally daily after specialist advice in her first trimester. The pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated; antenatal screening was unremarkable; the morphology scan at 20 weeks showed no structural anomalies. Birthweight is 3.4 kg, Apgars 8 and 9, cord pH 7.32. In the first 12 hours of life the neonate develops jitteriness, mild hypertonia, tremor, an irritable cry, and feeding difficulty with poor latch. Temperature, blood glucose (3.5 mmol/L), oxygen saturation (98 percent room air), and respiratory rate (52 breaths per minute) are all within normal ranges. Septic screen including FBC, CRP, blood culture is negative; cranial ultrasound is normal; no maternal opioid or other substance exposure is reported. The mother did not have gestational diabetes. There is no family history of metabolic disease. The neonatologist explains the most likely diagnosis to the mother.

data_table.Neonate: Jittery, hypertonic, tremulous, irritable cry, poor feeding from 12 hours of life
data_table.Vitals: T normal, glucose 3.5 mmol/L, SpO2 98%, RR 52
data_table.Workup: Septic screen negative, cranial USS normal, no substance exposure

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Neonatal hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose 3.5 is within normal range for term neonate (over 2.6); jitteriness is not glycaemic. Would be correct with BSL under 2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Neonatal abstinence syndrome

options.text: Early-onset neonatal sepsis with maternal chorioamnionitis as the underlying source
options.rationale: Septic screen negative, afebrile, normal sats/RR; sepsis remains differential but unlikely with this clinical picture. Would be correct with positive cultures (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Neonatal adaptation syndrome (poor neonatal adaptation) from late-trimester SSRI exposure
options.rationale: Neonatal adaptation syndrome (also called poor neonatal adaptation or post-natal adaptation syndrome) occurs in around 20-30 percent of neonates exposed to SSRIs in the third trimester, peaks within 48 hours, and resolves spontaneously within 1-2 weeks. Features include jitteriness, hypertonia, tremor, irritable cry, feeding difficulty, and occasionally transient respiratory distress. Paroxetine has the highest reported rate among SSRIs. Management is supportive observation in the special care nursery per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline.
source_refs: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline

options.text: Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy
options.rationale: HIE features sentinel hypoxic event, low Apgars, abnormal cord gases with depressed consciousness or seizures; her Apgars (8,9) and cord pH (7.32) are normal. Would be correct with low Apgars and abnormal cord gas (Source: ANZNN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Neonatal abstinence syndrome

options.text: Neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome
options.rationale: Opioid withdrawal requires maternal opioid exposure; none reported. Picture mimics neonatal adaptation but excluded by exposure history. Would be correct with opioid exposure (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Neonatal abstinence syndrome

explanation.summary: Neonatal adaptation syndrome is a transient, self-limited cluster of jitteriness, hypertonia, tremor, irritable cry, and feeding difficulty seen in 20-30 percent of neonates exposed to SSRIs in the third trimester. It peaks at 48 hours and resolves within 1-2 weeks with supportive care (COPE Guideline).
key_points: Onset within first 48 hours of life
Resolves spontaneously in 1-2 weeks
Higher rates with paroxetine and venlafaxine
Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn is a separate, rarer SSRI association (around 3-12 per 1000 vs 1-2 per 1000 baseline)
Not a reason to stop maternal SSRI during pregnancy if indicated
explanation.pearls: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn is a distinct rare SSRI association - do not confuse with neonatal adaptation syndrome (Australian Medicines Handbook).

sources.label: COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Neonatal abstinence syndrome

stem: A 25-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is maintained on sodium valproate 1000 mg orally daily and attends a Maitland community mental health clinic (Hunter New England LHD) for routine review. She has been euthymic for 18 months on valproate after two prior manic admissions; lithium was discontinued for tremor and polyuria with insufficient response, and olanzapine for metabolic side effects. She uses condoms inconsistently as her only contraception, is uncertain whether she wants children and has not actively planned a pregnancy. She has no other medications, normal renal and hepatic function, normal cycles and is not currently pregnant (LMP 10 days ago, urine beta-hCG negative). The TGA introduced the Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program in 2023 with mandatory annual acknowledgement, including documentation of pregnancy status, contraception adequacy, and risk acknowledgement.

data_table.Diagnosis: Bipolar I, euthymic 18 months on valproate 1000 mg daily
data_table.Pregnancy status: Not pregnant (beta-hCG negative, LMP 10 days ago)

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate plan under the TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program?

options.text: Continue current valproate unchanged because she is mood-stable today; document verbal acknowledgement of teratogenicity at her next routine visit
options.rationale: Stability does not exempt patient from PPP; TGA program is mandatory annually and requires written acknowledgement, documented contraception, and pregnancy testing. Would never be correct (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).

options.text: Cease valproate abruptly today with no replacement mood stabiliser, relying on her current stability until she returns when ready to actively conceive
options.rationale: Abrupt valproate cessation in patient with two prior manic admissions carries high relapse risk; planned switch to alternative needed. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

options.text: Switch immediately to topiramate as the safest pregnancy-compatible alternative without formal shared decision-making or PPP enrolment paperwork
options.rationale: Topiramate is also teratogenic (cleft palate, low birth weight) and not first-line for bipolar prophylaxis; choosing without contraception or shared decision-making fails PPP. Would be correct only with proper PPP adherence and contraception (Source: TGA).

options.text: In-clinic pregnancy test, counsel on the ~10% major malformation and IQ risks of valproate, ensure LARC, complete TGA risk acknowledgement annually, and formally review valproate vs alternatives
options.rationale: This is the complete TGA Valproate PPP step: document pregnancy status, written risk acknowledgement annually, contraception meeting the 'highly effective' threshold (LARC preferred since user-dependent methods like condoms are inadequate), and active reconsideration of indication. The PPP is an active program, not a passive form-filling exercise.

options.text: Recommend tubal ligation as the only acceptable option to continue valproate without formal pregnancy prevention program enrolment paperwork now
options.rationale: Tubal ligation is one option but NOT only acceptable; LARC (implant, IUD) meets PPP threshold and preserves fertility choice. Would be correct only if patient chooses tubal ligation (Source: TGA).

explanation.summary: The TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program mandates pregnancy testing, written risk acknowledgement, and highly effective contraception for every woman of childbearing potential on valproate. The 10 percent major malformation risk and 30-40 percent neurodevelopmental impact require active risk management.
key_points: Annual signed acknowledgement of risks required
Pregnancy test before initiation and at reviews
Highly effective contraception (LARC preferred) or two complementary methods
Major malformations ~10%, IQ loss ~7-10 points, ASD risk increased
Switch to lamotrigine (bipolar depression) or lithium (bipolar mania) when pregnancy planned
explanation.pearls: Valproate is the most teratogenic anticonvulsant - the PPP exists because risk management was historically inconsistent (TGA).

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders
